paragraphs.body: My name is Emily Davis, and I am a student majoring in Public Health in University of Pittsburgh. I recently read, “Are You Allowed to Criticize Simone Biles?” which focused on a central idea on Simone Biles’s struggle as a gymnast and the criticism she got when she wanted to step down from the Olympics for her mental health. I loved how you used the decision tree as a format for your essay because of how unique it is and how it can have a big impact on the readers allowing us to have a better idea of Simone Biles’s perspective and the perspective of the Americans who criticized her. I noticed how for each question that was asked you would give some background information about Simone Biles and then give straight answers of “yes” or “no” but for every “no” you would write about a paragraph showing how people can’t judge Simone Biles for what she’s going through or what she went through. And I thought it was impactful how for every time the answer was “no” you would criticize the Americans back for what they said when they don’t really know the struggle as a gymnast. I also liked how at the end you are giving Simone Biles comfort on how she can ignore what everyone is saying and how she is “the only person on Earth who is allowed to criticize [her].” While to the people who criticized her you just flipped them off on every “no”. Reading this allowed me to make connections to my life. I never went through what Simone Biles have gone through, but in school I’ve had moments where I was being criticized for something I haven’t done and made me feel guilty having some people turning their backs on me, but after reading your essay it made me feel a bit less guilty and how people can’t judge me when they don’t know the full truth about everything. I wonder what inspired you to use this format for your essay? It was very conversational for the targeted audience and easy to read while making it impactful. I also heard a lot of anger in this writing of how people should not be judging her for her decision, so I’m wondering why you’re writing this essay about Simone Biles or if you’ve ever gone through something similar as her causing a lot of tone in this writing. Thank you for sharing this essay that helped me get some comfort from my past and allowing people to understand how they can’t judge just from knowing a little bit of information about that person.

annotations.text: but in school I’ve had moments where I was being criticized for something I haven’t done and made me feel guilty having some people turning their backs on me, but after reading your essay it made me feel a bit less guilty and how people can’t judge me when they don’t know the full truth about everything.
annotations.comment: This is an excellent personal connection--I would be very interested to see you explore this even more, to share some of the concrete and specific experiences you have had.

This whole section is wonderful--you're doing a great job of developing this conversation with the writer, sharing your own observations with their work while being thorough about the details that the author has shared.

You've done a great job with this assignment! Your engagement with the author is impressive, and I'm really curious to see the details about your own experiences. While it's true that you're not Simone Biles, neither is the author, neither are the people they're addressing--your own observations are equally important and valid, and that is an important point to make!
